![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image001.png)

Jobro Agentic System PRD V2

3⽉25⽇修改

Jobro HK — Product Requirements Document

📋 1. Project Overview

One-sentence description: Jobro HK is an AI-powered job matching platform for students and graduates targeting Hong Kong employment. Users fill in basic preferences, upload a CV, and the AI proactively matches them with curated opportunities — delivered via the web platform and email notifications. Users can chat with the AI to refine preferences over time. 
 Problem Solved: Students and new grads who want to work in Hong Kong lack a centralized, high-signal platform for quality opportunities. They monitor fragmented sources — JobsDB, WeChat groups, social media, private group chats, university career portals, and company career pages — and live in constant fear of missing short-window opportunities. Existing platforms don't understand who they are beyond a resume, leading to poor matches and wasted effort. Target Users:

| •Primary User: Any student or recent graduate who wants to find a job or internship in Hong Kong. This includes: |                                                              |
| ------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| ◦                                                            | Students at top HK universities (HKU, HKUST, CUHK)           |
| ◦                                                            | Students at other HK universities (PolyU, CityU, HKBU, HKEdU, Lingnan, etc.) |
| ◦                                                            | Mainland Chinese students studying overseas who want to work  in HK |
| ◦                                                            | Overseas students (UK, US, Australia, etc.) targeting HK roles |
| ◦                                                            | Recent graduates already in HK looking for their next role   |
| •Cold-Start Strategy: Launch focused on HK market. MVP sign-up via email only. Recruit beta testers from university 树洞 (anonymous forums) — offer free 1-year accounts in exchange for 1v1 feedback sessions. Waitlist users from other regions and B-side (HR/recruiters). Expand geographically once sufficient waitlist volume is reached.  •Pricing Model: |                                                              |
| ◦                                                            | Free tier: 10 free job match pushes (one-time)               |
| ◦                                                            | Paid tier: ¥9.99/month (early bird pricing)                  |
| ◦                                                            | Referral program: Both referrer and referee get 1 free month |
| ◦                                                            | Beta testers: Free account for 1 year. Recruited from 树洞. Expected to  provide 1v1 feedback. |

🎯 2. Pain Points, Hypotheses & Success Metrics

Pain Points

![img](data:image/png;base64,)

Hypotheses

These are the core assumptions we're testing with the MVP. If any of these are wrong, the product needs to pivot.

![img](data:image/png;base64,)

MVP Success Metrics

Evaluation period: First 8 weeks after soft launch (~50 beta users, expanding to ~200). 
 Prerequisite — Event Tracking: All metrics below require in-product event tracking (see F7 in Feature List). Every key user action must be instrumented from day 1: sign-up, onboarding completion, match viewed, match clicked (apply / not interested), chat message sent, email opened, email clicked, referral sent, payment conversion, etc.

![img](data:image/png;base64,)

What "success" looks like after 8 weeks: If we hit activation >70%, WAU >40%, match acceptance >15%, week-4 retention >30%, and free→paid conversion >20%, the MVP is validated and we proceed to P1 (B-side + WeChat channels). If match quality is low despite good engagement, the matching algorithm needs work but the product concept is sound. If activation or retention are poor, we need to revisit the onboarding flow or value proposition.

⚡ 3. Feature List

🎯 P0 — MVP Core Features (Required for Launch)

F1. User Sign-Up + Profile Intake

•User signs up via email only (MVP)

•User uploads their CV (PDF) — required

•User can optionally add: LinkedIn URL, personal website, portfolio link, other links/files

•System parses CV to pre-populate structured profile fields

F2. Basic Preferences Form

•User fills in: 

◦Target roles (e.g., "Investment Banking Analyst", "Software Engineering Intern")

◦Availability: Number of days per week (for internships) + duration (e.g., 3 months, 6 months)

◦Salary expectation (optional)

◦Other practical filters: industry, company size, location within HK, etc.

F3. AI Job Matching + Notifications (Web + Email)

•On first login (after profile intake), the Jobro LLM is prompted with the user's info and finds matching JDs from the database

•Matched JDs are pushed to the web interface with an in-app notification. Full JD detail on web: company, role, location, requirements, responsibilities, salary, deadline, personalized rationale.

•User also receives email notifications with a condensed JD summary: company name, role title, location, key requirements, deadline — linking to the full JD on the web interface.

•Free tier: 10 free match pushes. After that, user must subscribe (¥9.99/month) to continue receiving matches.

•User sees a match feed on web with ranked job cards

•Each job card: user can click to apply (redirected to the original application website) or mark as "not interested" (LLM remembers and avoids similar jobs in future)

•Matching priority: User's stated intent first, supplemented by AI-inferred suggestions

F4. Text Chat with AI Agent

•User can text-chat with the Jobro LLM at any time

•User can say things like "I don't want consulting anymore" or "show me more fintech startups" or "actually I'm open to part-time too"

•LLM remembers all chat-based preference updates and applies them to future matching

•This is the primary mechanism for preference refinement (replaces voice onboarding)

F5. Curated Job Database

•Aggregated from tiered scraping strategy (see Appendix A for exhaustive source list)

•Supports Excel upload for manual/informal job sources (see Appendix A, Table 4)

•Jobs tagged with: industry, role type, seniority, deadline, source

F6. Pricing + Referral System

•Free tier: 10 free match pushes (one-time). No time limit — user can use them at their own pace.

•Paid tier: ¥9.99/month (early bird pricing). Unlimited match pushes + full chat access.

•Referral program: Both referrer and referee receive 1 free month of paid tier.

•Beta testers: Free account for 1 year. Recruited from university 树洞 forums. Expected to provide 1v1 feedback sessions.

F7. Event Tracking (Analytics)

•Instrument all key user actions from day 1 for product analytics and success metric tracking
 •Tracked events include: sign-up, onboarding step completion, CV upload, preferences saved, match viewed, match clicked (apply), match rejected (not interested), chat message sent, email opened, email click-through, referral link generated, referral converted, payment initiated, payment completed, session start/end, page views•Use a lightweight analytics pipeline (e.g., Mixpanel, Amplitude, or custom event logging to PostgreSQL)

•Required for all MVP success metrics (see Section 2)

✨ P1 — B-Side + WeChat Channels (Post-MVP)

F8. WeChat ⼩程序 (Mini Program) Notifications — Users bind their account to the Jobro ⼩程序 to receive match notifications directly in WeChat (condensed JD summary linking to full web JD)

F9. WeCom (企业微信) Community Channel — Jobro posts 朋友圈 (Moments) with career tips; users invited to private group chats with exclusive, manually curated JDs

F10. WeChat / Phone Number Auth — Additional sign-up methods beyond email

F11. AI Thinking Process Display — Agent shows its reasoning process when matching or chatting (similar to Claude's extended thinking), so users can see why a job was recommended and build trust in the AI

F12. Employer Onboarding — Companies post jobs directly on Jobro instead of external sites

F13. One-Click Apply on Platform — Applicants apply within Jobro (no redirect to external sites)

F14. Mutual Interest Matching — If both candidate and employer click "interested," the platform facilitates interview scheduling

F15. Employer Dashboard — Company-side view of applicant pool, shortlisting, and communication

🔮 P2 — China Mainland Expansion

F16. Geographic expansion to mainland China job market

F17. Mainland-specific job sources and scraping

F18. Localization for mainland platforms and user expectations

🚀 P3 — Voice Onboarding + Advanced Features

F19. 20-Minute Voice Onboarding Conversation — AI agent conducts structured voice conversation to build deep candidate profile (see Appendix B for full framework)

F20. RIASEC + Big Five + Career Values assessment (integrated into voice conversation)

F21. Personality-based discovery suggestions (20–30% of matches based on assessment, not just stated intent)

F22. Peer Signal Layer — Anonymized application signals

F23. "Warm Intro" Matching — Alumni/peer connections

F24. Cantonese Voice Support

F25. Ghost Job Detection, Application Tracker, Resume & Interview Coaching

F26. A2A (Agent-to-Agent) Matching — Candidate and employer agents negotiate autonomously

🚶 4. User Flow

Main Scenario: New User Onboarding → First Match

1.Landing page → "Stop refreshing 10 platforms. Let your AI career agent find opportunities for you."

2.Sign up → Email only (MVP)

3.CV upload → User uploads CV (PDF). System parses and extracts key info. Optional: add LinkedIn URL, personal website, portfolio, other files.

4.Preferences form → User fills in: target roles, days per week + duration (for internships), salary (optional), industry preferences

5.First match feed → System immediately runs matching. User sees initial job cards on the web interface, ranked by relevance. Each card: title, company, location, key requirements, deadline, personalized rationale. (First 10 pushes are free.)

6.Apply or reject → User clicks a job to apply (redirected to original application site) or marks "not interested" (AI remembers and avoids similar jobs).

7.Ongoing email notifications → New matches pushed to web interface + email notification with condensed summary (company, role, location, deadline) linking to full JD on web.

8.Ongoing chat refinement → User can text the AI agent anytime: "no more consulting," "show me more startups," "I'm also open to part-time." AI remembers and updates matching.

9.Paywall after 10 free pushes → User prompted to subscribe (¥9.99/month) or refer a friend for 1 free month.

Matching Logic

The matching engine respects user intent first:

1.Primary filter — stated preferences: Target roles, availability, salary range, industry. The majority of matches follow what the user explicitly asked for.

2.CV-inferred signals: Skills, experience, education parsed from CV supplement the explicit preferences — e.g., if user has 2 finance internships but also says "open to tech," the AI can weight accordingly.

3.Chat-based refinement: Every text message to the AI updates the user's preference model. "Not interested" signals are treated as negative training data — the AI learns what to avoid.



![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image005.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image006.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image007.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image008.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image009.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image010.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image010.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image013.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image014.png)

4.No personality assessment in MVP. RIASEC/Big Five/Values assessment is deferred to voice onboarding in P3 (Phase 4). MVP matching relies entirely on stated preferences + CV + feedback signals.

🎨 5. UI/UX Direction

Style: Minimalist and professional. Clean, confident, no clutter.

Visual Feel: Premium, trustworthy, modern, understated. Typography-driven. Muted palette + one accent color. Mobile-first.

References: Linear (clean interface), Notion (simple onboarding), Perplexity (AI-generated results presentation)

🛠 6. Recommended Technical Solution

Platform

PWA → migrate to native via Capacitor (React Native ecosystem) when ready.

Tech Stack (AWS-Native)

![img](data:image/png;base64,)

LLM Stack — See Appendix D for detailed model comparison

Note: Voice pipeline (ASR + TTS) is deferred to P3 (Phase 4). MVP uses text-only LLM interaction (Qwen3 on Bedrock for matching + chat).

📅 7. Development Phases

Phase 1: MVP — Text-First Matching Platform (4 weeks)

![img](data:image/png;base64,)

Phase 2 (P1): B-Side + WeChat Channels + Enhancements (Months 2–5)

•WeChat ⼩程序 notification binding (condensed JD push via WeChat)

•WeCom 企业微信 community channel (Moments + private group chats with exclusive JDs)

•WeChat / phone number auth (additional sign-up methods)

•AI thinking process display (agent shows reasoning behind matches, similar to Claude's extended thinking)

•Employer onboarding: companies post jobs directly on Jobro

•One-click apply within platform (no external redirect)

•Mutual interest matching: both sides click "interested" → platform schedules interview

•Employer dashboard: applicant pool, shortlisting, communication

•PMF testing, word-of-mouth growth

Phase 3 (P2): China Mainland Expansion (Months 6–10)

•Mainland-specific job sources and scraping

•Localization for mainland platforms and user expectations

•Geographic expansion of matching engine

•Regulatory compliance for mainland operations

Phase 4 (P3): Voice Onboarding + Advanced Features (Months 11+)

  •20-minute voice onboarding conversation (see Appendix B for framework)  •RIASEC + Big Five + Career Values assessment integrated into voice  •Personality-based discovery suggestions (supplement intent-based matching)  •Voice pipeline: ASR → LLM → TTS (see Appendix D for stack)  •Cantonese voice support  •Advanced features: peer signals, warm intros, ghost job detection, A2A matching  

APPENDIX A: Job Scraping Strategy

Approach: Scrape First, Partner Later

All sources are scraped initially. As user base grows, transition to formal data partnerships with university career centers and platforms.

Note on overseas/mainland students: These users won't find HK jobs on their home university portals. For them, the value of Jobro is precisely that it aggregates HK-specific sources they can't easily access. All sources below list jobs located in Hong Kong.

Table 1: High Priority Sources (Build These First)

These 7 sources cover ~90% of HK student/grad jobs with minimal overlap.

Table 2: Lower Priority Sources (Add Incrementally)

These overlap heavily with Table 1 sources or have access/legal complications. Add as capacity allows.

Table 3: Company Career Pages (High Quality, Low Volume)

Scrape career pages of top employers weekly. See main PRD for full list by industry (Finance, Consulting, Tech, Startups, Government). These are all public pages with low legal risk.

Scan frequency: 1x weekly for all company career pages.

Table 4: Manual / Informal Sources (Excel Upload)

These sources cannot be scraped automatically. They are often the highest-signal, shortest-window opportunities (e.g., a recruiter posts in a WeChat group that closes in 48 hours).

Workflow: Team members (or community volunteers) collect job postings from these channels and upload them via a standardized Excel template (.xlsx). The system parses the Excel file and logs the jobs into the backend database.

Excel template columns:

![img](data:image/png;base64,)

Sources for manual upload:

![img](data:image/png;base64,)

Upload frequency: As needed, ideally 2–3x per week. Target ~50 high-signal manual postings per month.

APPENDIX B: 20-Minute Voice Conversation Framework (Phase 4 — Future Reference)

Note: Voice onboarding is deferred to Phase 4. This appendix is preserved as the design spec for when voice is implemented. In MVP, the equivalent user understanding is built through: CV parsing + preferences form + ongoing text chat with the AI agent.

Design Principles

•Tone: Smart recruiter friend — casual, peer-like, warm but direct

•Languages: English or Mandarin (user selects). Code-switching supported.

•Feel: NOT a survey. Should feel like a genuine conversation where the agent is curious about the user.

•Assessment integration: RIASEC, Big Five (4 dims), and Career Values assessed implicitly. User never feels "tested."

Conversation Structure

Part 1: Warm-up & Career Context (3 minutes)

Goal: Build rapport. Understand career stage, targets, and motivation.

Agent opening example:

"Hey! Nice to meet you. I've had a look at your resume — [personalized comment]. Before we dive in, I'm curious — what's the story? What are you looking for right now, and what's driving the search?"

Topics: Career stage, target roles/industries/companies, timeline, motivation.

Assessment signals: Explicit career targets; early Values signals (prestige? impact? money?).

Part 2: Career Interest Profiling — RIASEC (5 minutes)

Goal: Determine Holland Code through natural scenario-based questions.

Sample questions:

•"If you had a free Saturday, would you build something, research something, or organize something?"

•"In group projects, are you the creative ideas person or the one who makes sure everything ships on time?"



![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image019.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image020.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image021.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image022.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image023.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image024.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image025.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image026.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image027.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image028.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image029.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image030.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image031.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image032.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image033.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image034.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image035.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image036.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image037.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image038.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image039.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image040.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image041.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image042.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image001.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image043.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image001.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image044.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image045.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image046.png)![img](file:////Users/broccoli/Library/Group%20Containers/UBF8T346G9.Office/TemporaryItems/msohtmlclip/clip_image047.png)

•"What draws you to [stated target] — the problem-solving, client interaction, variety, or something else?"

RIASEC mapping: Realistic (hands-on), Investigative (analytical), Artistic (creative), Social (helping), Enterprising (leadership), Conventional (organized). Score 0–100 per dimension from cumulative patterns.

Part 3: Personality Signals — Big Five (4 minutes)

Goal: Assess Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Openness, Agreeableness through behavior questions + passive observation of communication style.

Sample questions:

•Conscientiousness: "Walk me through how you prepared for your last exam or project deadline."

•Extraversion: "Do you recharge with friends or alone time after a long day?"

•Openness: "If you could switch your major tomorrow with no consequences, what would you pick?"

•Agreeableness: "Tell me about a time you disagreed with someone in a group. How did you handle it?"

Neuroticism excluded — sensitive, not useful for student job matching.

Part 4: Career Values Exploration (4 minutes)

Goal: Understand what drives career satisfaction through forced trade-offs.

Part 5: Practical Preferences & Wrap-up (4 minutes)

Topics: Salary floor, location, company size, deal-breakers, timeline, anything else.

Agent closing example:

"I've got a solid picture of you. Let me play it back: [2-sentence summary]. Sound right? Great — I'll start looking. You'll hear from me within a couple of days."

Assessment Output

Produces structured CandidateProfile with: RIASEC scores (6D), Big Five (4D), Career Values (6D), personality narrative, explicit preferences, and embedding vector.

APPENDIX C: Assessment Framework — Scales and Criteria (Phase 4 — Future Reference)

Note: Formal RIASEC/Big Five/Values assessment is deferred to Phase 4 (integrated into voice onboarding). MVP matching relies on stated preferences + CV signals + chat feedback. This appendix is preserved as the design spec for when the assessment is implemented.

Framework 1: Holland RIASEC

Public domain. Most widely validated career interest framework. Used by O*NET (900+ occupations).

Matching: Each JD is RIASEC-classified by AI. Cosine similarity between candidate and JD RIASEC vectors.

Framework 2: Big Five (4 of 5 Dimensions)

Framework 3: Career Values (6 Dimensions)

APPENDIX D: LLM Stack — Model Recommendations

Note: Voice pipeline (ASR + TTS) is deferred to Phase 4. This appendix covers the LLM models used for text-based matching and chat in MVP, plus the voice stack design for future reference.

LLM Models on Amazon Bedrock

Since you're on AWS Activate, all Bedrock models are covered by credits. Here are non-Claude alternatives with strong Chinese language capabilities:

Recommendation: Use Qwen3-235B as your primary conversation engine. It's built by Alibaba with native Chinese language capabilities — far superior to any Western model for Mandarin conversations. Use Qwen3-32B for batch/lightweight tasks. Both are fully managed and serverless on Bedrock, covered by AWS Activate credits.

Why Qwen over Claude for this use case: Claude is an excellent model overall, but for a product where the primary interaction is a 20-minute conversation that may be entirely in Mandarin, native Chinese language quality is paramount. Qwen3 was built with Chinese as a first-class language, not an afterthought. The difference in Mandarin fluency, idiom handling, and cultural context understanding is meaningful.

Speech Services

ASR (Speech-to-Text)

TTS (Text-to-Speech)

Unified Speech-to-Speech (Alternative)

Recommended Voice Pipeline (Phase 4 — Future Reference)

MVP (English + Mandarin):

代码块

| 1  2  3  4 | User speaks → Amazon Transcribe  (streaming ASR)    → Text to Qwen3-235B on Bedrock  (conversation + assessment)    → Response text to Amazon Polly (neural TTS:  EN voices / Zhiyu for Mandarin)    → Audio streamed back to user |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
|            |                                                              |

Phase 2 (add Cantonese):

代码块

| 1  2  3  4 | User speaks Cantonese → Tencent  Cloud ASR (streaming)    → Text to Qwen3-235B on Bedrock (handles  Cantonese text natively)    → Response text to Amazon Polly Hiujin  (Cantonese neural TTS)    → Audio streamed back to user |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
|            |                                                              |

Mandarin Voice Quality Consideration

Amazon Polly's Mandarin voice (Zhiyu) is neural and decent, but only has one female voice. If during testing it doesn't sound natural enough for a 20-minute peer-like conversation, consider:

1.Tencent Cloud TTS — wider Mandarin voice selection, more natural for conversational use. Would need to pay outside AWS credits.

2.CAMB AI MARS6 — first voice model on Bedrock Marketplace. May have better Mandarin voices. Evaluate during development.

3.Custom Amazon Polly Brand Voice — Amazon offers custom voice creation if you want a unique Jobro voice. Requires more upfront work.

Test Polly Zhiyu first. If quality is insufficient for the conversational tone you want, Tencent Cloud TTS is the recommended alternative for Mandarin.

Cost Estimation (Monthly, 500 Users)

MVP (Text-Only — no voice)

Significantly lower than v3 estimate (~$410/mo) because voice pipeline (Transcribe ~$240 + Polly ~$40) is deferred. All covered by AWS Activate credits at MVP scale.

Phase 4 Addition (Voice Onboarding)
